General Principles
In general, a criminal offense involves three elements:

rules.canonical_name: Actus Reus – A Wrongful Act Or Conduct
rules.rule_statement: ii) Mens rea – an individual’s mental state or guilty mind; and iii) Causation – a causal connection between the act or conduct and the result. Modern criminal statutes prescribe the type of actus reus and mens rea that must be proved by the prosecution beyond a reasonable doubt for criminal liability. There is an exception to this general rule for strict-liability crimes. Strict-liability crimes do not have a mens rea requirement.

rules.rule_statement: Rule Before there can be a crime, there must be a criminal act—actus reus. The criminal act must typically be a voluntary, affirmative act that causes a criminally proscribed result. The act requirement may also be satisfied by an “omission” or failure to act under circumstances imposing a legal duty to act.

Actus Reus—Acts And Omissions
Rule Before there can be a crime, there must be a criminal act—actus reus. The criminal act must typically be a voluntary, affirmative act that causes a criminally proscribed result. The act requirement may also be satisfied by an “omission” or failure to act under circumstances imposing a legal duty to act.

rules.rule_statement: In general, a criminal act must be a physical act. A bad thought standing alone cannot result in criminal liability.
rules.short_rule_statement: In general, a criminal act must be a physical act. A bad thought standing alone cannot result in criminal liability.
rules.source_text:
Physical Act
In general, a criminal act must be a physical act. A bad thought standing alone cannot result in criminal liability.

rules.rule_statement: Speech can constitute a physical act. For example, in the crime of extortion, a verbal or written threat may serve as the required act. A mere statement of intent generally would not be enough to constitute an actus reus, but speech that encourages someone to commit a crime could be a criminal act.

Speech
Speech can constitute a physical act. For example, in the crime of extortion, a verbal or written threat may serve as the required act. A mere statement of intent generally would not be enough to constitute an actus reus, but speech that encourages someone to commit a crime could be a criminal act.

rules.rule_statement: Possession of an object can constitute a physical act. Generally, a person must have conscious knowledge of the possession but does not need to know that such possession is illegal. Under the MPC, possession can be a criminal act only if the defendant knew she had possession of the object and was aware of such possession for a sufficient period of time to have been able to terminate the possession.

Possession
Possession of an object can constitute a physical act. Generally, a person must have conscious knowledge of the possession but does not need to know that such possession is illegal. Under the MPC, possession can be a criminal act only if the defendant knew she had possession of the object and was aware of such possession for a sufficient period of time to have been able to terminate the possession.

rules.rule_statement: A criminal act must be physical and voluntary. Actions during unconsciousness, sleep, or hypnosis are not voluntary. The best example of when liability is not generally imposed is for acts committed while sleepwalking. Other acts that are not considered voluntary are reflexive or convulsive acts and conduct that is not the product of a person’s self-determination. Example: Person A pushed Person B into a bystander, injuring the bystander. Person B cannot be held criminally liable for the act of injuring the bystander because Person B’s conduct was not voluntary. By way of further example, a person with epilepsy may still be criminally responsible if (i) the person knows of the possibility of seizure and (ii) the person’s last act was voluntary. Example: Person C knows that she has been diagnosed with epilepsy and is prone to seizures. If Person C had a seizure while driving and caused a fatal accident, Person C can be held criminally responsible for any injuries or fatalities resulting from the accident.

rules.rule_statement: A legal duty to act and the failure to do so results in criminal liability in these five instances: ii) Contract (e.g., a lifeguard’s obligation to save a drowning person, a childcare provider’s obligation to care for a child); iii) Special relationship (e.g., a parent’s duty to a child, a person’s duty to a spouse); iv) Detrimental undertaking (e.g., leaving a victim in worse condition after treatment); and The defendant must have knowledge of the facts giving rise to the duty to act and yet fail to act in accordance with the duty. Additionally, it must be reasonably possible for the defendant to perform the duty.

Additionally, it must be reasonably possible for the defendant to perform the duty.

rules.rule_statement: When there is no duty to act, a defendant is not criminally liable for failing to help others in trouble. A mere bystander has no duty to act. EXAM NOTE: No matter how unsympathetic the examiners may make a bystander seem for not acting to help someone, there is generally no legal duty to act when the bystander had no involvement with the peril. MPC § 2.01(4).

rules.rule_statement: Rule Mens rea is the requirement of a guilty mind or legally proscribed mental state that a defendant must possess to commit a crime. Except for strict-liability crimes, a crime is committed when a criminal act (actus reus) is coupled with a guilty mind. Both the physical and mental elements must exist at the same time—“concurrence.” Strict-liability crimes have no mens rea requirement and require only an actus reus. The MPC defines mens rea according to four levels of culpability: Purposely Knowingly or willfully Recklessly Negligently As a general matter, when evaluating the issue of whether a defendant had the requisite mental state, you should first carefully review the criminal statute for the type of intent required for the alleged offense. EXAM NOTE: When you are tested on a section of the MPC or any other criminal statute, read the section carefully for hints about the mens rea requirement.  Look for words like “with intent” (speciﬁc-intent crime) or “knowingly” or “recklessly” (general-intent crime).  If mens rea is not speciﬁed in the section, then the offense may be a strict-liability crime.

rules.rule_statement: Specific-intent crimes include first-degree murder, inchoate offenses, assault with intent to commit a battery, and theft offenses. Specific-intent crimes require that the defendant possess a subjective desire, specific objective, or knowledge to accomplish a prohibited result. When dealing with specific-intent crimes, it is necessary to identify specific intent for two reasons. First, the prosecution must prove specific intent to prosecute the defendant. Second, certain defenses (e.g., voluntary intoxication and unreasonable mistake of fact) are applicable only to specific-intent crimes. EXAM NOTE: A simple way to remember the common-law crimes that require speciﬁc intent is by using the mnemonic FIAT: First-degree murder Inchoate offenses- attempt, solicitation, and conspiracy Assault with the intent to commit battery Theft offenses- larceny, larceny by trick, false pretenses, embezzlement, forgery, burglary, and robbery Whenever a fact pattern deﬁnes the crime as requiring “the intent to…,” the crime is a speciﬁc-intent crime.

rules.rule_statement: The crimes of common-law murder and arson require malice—reckless disregard of a high risk of harm. Malice does not require the defendant to act with ill will toward the victim. A defendant who acts with greater culpability (i.e., intent) can be criminally liable for a malice crime. Although the malice crimes (common-law murder and arson) appear to have an “intent” requirement (e.g., intent to kill, intent to burn), malice only requires a criminal act without excuse, justification, or mitigation. Intent can be inferred from the accomplishment of the act.

rules.source_text:
Malice Crimes
The crimes of common-law murder and arson require malice—reckless disregard of a high risk of harm. Malice does not require the defendant to act with ill will toward the victim. A defendant who acts with greater culpability (i.e., intent) can be criminally liable for a malice crime. Although the malice crimes (common-law murder and arson) appear to have an “intent” requirement (e.g., intent to kill, intent to burn), malice only requires a criminal act without excuse, justification, or mitigation. Intent can be inferred from the accomplishment of the act.

rules.rule_statement: General-intent crimes require only the intent to perform an unlawful act. General-intent crimes have traditionally included battery, rape, kidnapping, and false imprisonment. Motive is the reason or explanation for committing a crime and is immaterial to the substantive criminal offense.  It is different from a person’s intent to perform an act.

rules.rule_statement: When a defendant acts “purposely,” the conscious objective is to engage in the conduct or to cause a certain result.
rules.short_rule_statement: When a defendant acts “purposely,” the conscious objective is to engage in the conduct or to cause a certain result.
rules.source_text:
Purposely
When a defendant acts “purposely,” the conscious objective is to engage in the conduct or to cause a certain result.

rules.rule_statement: “Knowingly” or “willfully” requires that a defendant be aware that the conduct is of the nature required by the crime or that the circumstances required by the crime exist. In other words, the defendant must be aware or know that the result is practically certain to occur based on the conduct.

Knowingly Or Willfully
“Knowingly” or “willfully” requires that a defendant be aware that the conduct is of the nature required by the crime or that the circumstances required by the crime exist. In other words, the defendant must be aware or know that the result is practically certain to occur based on the conduct.

rules.rule_statement: “Recklessly” requires a defendant to act with a conscious disregard of a substantial and unjustifiable risk that a material element of a crime exists or will result from the conduct. The risk must constitute a gross deviation from the standard of conduct of a law-abiding person. Mere realization of a risk is not enough.

Recklessly
“Recklessly” requires a defendant to act with a conscious disregard of a substantial and unjustifiable risk that a material element of a crime exists or will result from the conduct. The risk must constitute a gross deviation from the standard of conduct of a law-abiding person. Mere realization of a risk is not enough.

rules.rule_statement: A defendant acts “negligently” when that defendant should be aware of a substantial and unjustifiable risk that a material element of a crime exists or will result from the conduct. The risk must constitute a gross deviation from the standard of care of a reasonable person in the same situation. Furthermore, as with tort law, a violation of a criminal statute or ordinance may be evidence of negligent conduct.

Negligently
A defendant acts “negligently” when that defendant should be aware of a substantial and unjustifiable risk that a material element of a crime exists or will result from the conduct. The risk must constitute a gross deviation from the standard of care of a reasonable person in the same situation. Furthermore, as with tort law, a violation of a criminal statute or ordinance may be evidence of negligent conduct.

rules.rule_statement: The MPC mental states are ordered from negligence as the lowest degree of fault to purposefully as the highest level of fault. Consequently, if a criminal statute specifies a mental state, proof of a more culpable mental state satisfies the mens rea requirement. For example, if a statutory crime requires that an act be undertaken knowingly, establishing that the act was committed purposefully satisfies the mens rea requirement for that act.

rules.source_text:
Hierarchy Of Mental States
The MPC mental states are ordered from negligence as the lowest degree of fault to purposefully as the highest level of fault. Consequently, if a criminal statute specifies a mental state, proof of a more culpable mental state satisfies the mens rea requirement. For example, if a statutory crime requires that an act be undertaken knowingly, establishing that the act was committed purposefully satisfies the mens rea requirement for that act.

rules.rule_statement: If the requisite mental state is not specified in a criminal statute, then the mens rea requirement will be satisfied when the defendant acted at least recklessly. If the mens rea requirement is not listed for each material element of the crime, then the mental state applicable to one material element is applicable to all other material elements, unless a contrary purpose plainly appears.

Mental State Not Speciﬁed

If the mens rea requirement is not listed for each material element of the crime, then the mental state applicable to one material element is applicable to all other material elements, unless a contrary purpose plainly appears.

rules.rule_statement: Strict-liability crimes do not require a criminal mental state (mens rea). A criminal act (actus reus) is sufficient for a strict-liability crime. Examples of strict-liability crimes include statutory rape, bigamy, and public-welfare offenses.

rules.source_text:
Strict-Liability Crimes
Strict-liability crimes do not require a criminal mental state (mens rea). A criminal act (actus reus) is sufficient for a strict-liability crime. Examples of strict-liability crimes include statutory rape, bigamy, and public-welfare offenses.

rules.rule_statement: A public-welfare offense is a strict-liability crime for which no mens rea is required. Conduct that is subject to stringent public regulation includes that which could seriously threaten the public’s health or safety or is inherently dangerous. Typical examples of public-welfare offenses include adulteration of food or drugs, regulation of waste disposal, regulation of firearms, and sale of alcohol to minors.

Typical examples of public-welfare offenses include adulteration of food or drugs, regulation of waste disposal, regulation of firearms, and sale of alcohol to minors.

rules.rule_statement: Criminal offenses requiring no mens rea are generally disfavored. Thus, courts have traditionally held that there must be some clear indication of congressional intent, express or implied, to dispense with mens rea as an element of the crime. In determining legislative intent, courts will often consider the severity of the associated penalty, finding crimes with relatively light penalties to be strict- liability offenses and those with more severe penalties (such as felony crimes) to have a mens rea requirement.

Presumption Against Strict Liability
Criminal offenses requiring no mens rea are generally disfavored. Thus, courts have traditionally held that there must be some clear indication of congressional intent, express or implied, to dispense with mens rea as an element of the crime. In determining legislative intent, courts will often consider the severity of the associated penalty, finding crimes with relatively light penalties to be strict- liability offenses and those with more severe penalties (such as felony crimes) to have a mens rea requirement.

rules.rule_statement: In general, the MPC imposes a state-of-mind requirement for material elements of a crime. However, the MPC does not impose a state-of-mind requirement for rape of a child under the age of 10. Instead, the MPC recognizes such an offense as a strict-liability crime. In addition, the MPC does not require proof of culpability for an offense whose penalty is limited to a fine.

Instead, the MPC recognizes such an offense as a strict-liability crime. In addition, the MPC does not require proof of culpability for an offense whose penalty is limited to a fine.

rules.rule_statement: Vicarious-liability crimes do not require the defendant to commit a criminal act (actus reus). Instead, criminal liability is imposed on the defendant for the criminal act of a third party. Vicarious liability differs from strict liability because vicarious-liability crimes do not require an actus reus by the defendant. Instead, vicarious liability holds a defendant criminally responsible for the actus reus of a third party. By contrast, a strict-liability crime requires a defendant to commit a personal act (actus reus) and does not impose liability for a defendant’s conduct on a third party.

Vicarious liability differs from strict liability because vicarious-liability crimes do not require an actus reus by the defendant. Instead, vicarious liability holds a defendant criminally responsible for the actus reus of a third party. By contrast, a strict-liability crime requires a defendant to commit a personal act (actus reus) and does not impose liability for a defendant’s conduct on a third party.

rules.rule_statement: Courts often impose vicarious liability for strict?liability crimes, most commonly when an employer or principal is vicariously liable for the crimes of an employee or agent. However, sometimes it is unclear whether the legislature intended for vicarious liability to apply to a strict-liability offense. When the punishment for a strict-liability crime is light, courts are more likely to find that vicarious liability applies. The modern trend is to limit vicarious liability to regulatory crimes.

Application To Strict-Liability Crimes

However, sometimes it is unclear whether the legislature intended for vicarious liability to apply to a strict-liability offense. When the punishment for a strict-liability crime is light, courts are more likely to find that vicarious liability applies. The modern trend is to limit vicarious liability to regulatory crimes.

rules.rule_statement: Vicarious liability may present due process issues because it can involve criminal liability without a personal act on the part of the defendant. Although imprisonment for a faultless crime may have constitutional due process implications, the application of vicarious liability in cases with fines only is unlikely to constitute a denial of due process.

On the other hand, modern statutes impose vicarious liability on corporations when the offensive act is performed by an agent of the corporation acting within the scope of employment. Additionally, modern statutes also impose liability on a corporation for acts performed by a high-ranking corporate agent who likely represents corporate policy. rules.source_text:
Application To Corporations
At common law, corporations did not face criminal liability because an entity (unlike a person) could not form the necessary mens rea. On the other hand, modern statutes impose vicarious liability on corporations when the offensive act is performed by an agent of the corporation acting within the scope of employment. Additionally, modern statutes also impose liability on a corporation for acts performed by a high-ranking corporate agent who likely represents corporate policy. Under the MPC, a corporation may be held criminally liable when:

rules.canonical_name: The Corporation Fails To Discharge A Specific Duty Imposed By Law
rules.rule_statement: ii) The board of directors or a high-ranking agent of the corporation acting within the scope of his employment authorizes or recklessly tolerates the offensive act; or iii) The legislative purpose statutorily imposes liability on a corporation for a specific act. The individual agent of the corporation who violated the statute may also be held criminally liable, and the corporation’s conviction does not preclude conviction of the individual.

rules.rule_statement: The defendant’s conduct must cause the particular unlawful result or circumstance. Causation means that the defendant’s mens rea (if required) caused the criminal act (actus reus). The act must cause the particular result made unlawful by the statute. See § II.A.2. rules.rule_statement: There are two types of mistakes that may serve as a defense, depending on the type of crime: mistake of fact and mistake of law.
rules.short_rule_statement: There are two types of mistakes that may serve as a defense, depending on the type of crime: mistake of fact and mistake of law.
rules.source_text:
Mistake As A Defense
There are two types of mistakes that may serve as a defense, depending on the type of crime: mistake of fact and mistake of law.

rules.rule_statement: Mistake of fact is a defendant’s misunderstanding of a material fact. Mistake of fact may negate criminal intent, but it must be an honest mistake. The defense applies differently between specific and general-intent crimes. Mistake of fact is never a defense to a strict-liability crime because strict-liability offenses do not have a mens rea requirement. EXAM NOTE: When considering a mistake-of-fact defense, analyze whether the alleged crime requires a showing of speciﬁc intent, general intent, or strict liability.

Mistake Of Fact
Mistake of fact is a defendant’s misunderstanding of a material fact. Mistake of fact may negate criminal intent, but it must be an honest mistake. The defense applies differently between specific and general-intent crimes. Mistake of fact is never a defense to a strict-liability crime because strict-liability offenses do not have a mens rea requirement. EXAM NOTE: When considering a mistake-of-fact defense, analyze whether the alleged crime requires a showing of speciﬁc intent, general intent, or strict liability.

rules.rule_statement: A mistake of fact is a defense to a specific-intent crime even if the mistake was unreasonable. Example: An athlete took an expensive gold watch from a table mistakenly thinking that it was her inexpensive plastic sports watch. Even though the athlete’s mistake of fact was unreasonable, the athlete lacked the intent to steal necessary for larceny—a specific- intent crime.

Speciﬁc-Intent Crime
A mistake of fact is a defense to a specific-intent crime even if the mistake was unreasonable. Example: An athlete took an expensive gold watch from a table mistakenly thinking that it was her inexpensive plastic sports watch. Even though the athlete’s mistake of fact was unreasonable, the athlete lacked the intent to steal necessary for larceny—a specific- intent crime.

rules.rule_statement: A mistake of fact must be reasonable to be a defense to a general-intent or malice crime. Under the MPC, a mistake or ignorance of fact that negates the required state of mind for a material element of a crime is a defense. The mistake or ignorance of fact effectively prevents the prosecution from establishing that element. Since a strict-liability crime does not require intent, malice, or a specific mental state, mistake of fact is not a defense to such a crime. When a mistake of fact would serve to negate the required intent or state of mind, the defendant bears the burden of offering evidence of the mistake (i.e., the burden of production), but the burden of proof as to existence of the required intent or state of mind remains with the prosecution.

General-Intent And Malice Crimes
A mistake of fact must be reasonable to be a defense to a general-intent or malice crime. Under the MPC, a mistake or ignorance of fact that negates the required state of mind for a material element of a crime is a defense. The mistake or ignorance of fact effectively prevents the prosecution from establishing that element. Since a strict-liability crime does not require intent, malice, or a specific mental state, mistake of fact is not a defense to such a crime. When a mistake of fact would serve to negate the required intent or state of mind, the defendant bears the burden of offering evidence of the mistake (i.e., the burden of production), but the burden of proof as to existence of the required intent or state of mind remains with the prosecution.

rules.rule_statement: Mistake of law is a defendant’s misunderstanding of the legal effect of a known fact or circumstance. At common law, mistake or ignorance of the law generally is not a valid defense even if the mistake is reasonable. Under the MPC, a defendant’s state of mind regarding the existence, meaning, or application of a law or whether conduct constitutes a crime is generally not an element of the crime. Consequently, a defendant’s lack of knowledge of a criminal statute is not a defense to the crime. However, as with a mistake of fact, a defendant’s honestly held mistake of law may negate the mental state (e.g., knowledge) required for a material element of the crime or establish a defense provided for in the law. Example: Party A forcibly took money from Party B to settle B’s debt to A. Party A had the mistaken belief that the law allows for self-help in such situations. Subsequently, A was charged with robbery, which requires a defendant to act with the purpose of depriving another of his property. Because A’s mistaken belief negated the purposefulness element required for the crime of robbery, A is not criminally liable.

rules.rule_statement: The transferred-intent rule is also known as the unintended victim rule. When a defendant acts with an intent to cause harm to one person or object and that defendant’s act directly results in harm to another person or object (i.e., unintended victim), the defendant can be criminally liable for the harm caused under the doctrine of transferred intent. Example: Party D pointed a gun at Party A, intending to shoot and kill A, but accidentally shot and killed B instead. Party D is guilty of two crimes: the murder of B under the doctrine of transferred intent and the attempted murder of A. Note that the doctrine of transferred intent applies only to “bad aim” cases and not to cases of mistaken identity. Example: Party D aimed at and shot Party A. Even though D mistakenly believed Party A was Party B, D is nonetheless guilty of shooting A. Because D’s bullet hit the body (A) that D intended to shoot, intent does not need to be transferred for criminal liability to attach to D’s actions.

rules.rule_statement: The transferred-intent doctrine is usually confined to homicide, battery, and arson. Any defenses that the defendant could assert against the intended victim (e.g., self-defense) may also transfer to the unintended victim. Note that transferred intent does not apply to attempted crimes, only completed crimes. Example: Party D shot at Party A with the intent to kill A, but D missed and shot B. The shot merely injured B. Party D can be convicted of the attempted murder of A and of battery against B but cannot be convicted of the attempted murder of B.

rules.rule_statement: Under the U.S. Constitution, neither federal nor state governments may criminalize conduct that has already occurred (i.e., an ex post facto law) or impose punishment without a trial (i.e., a bill of attainder). The Due Process Clauses of the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments prevent both federal and state governments from imposing criminal liability without giving clear warnings as to the conduct prohibited. Additionally, many jurisdictions are in the process of eliminating multiple convictions against a defendant with more than one offense when those multiple offenses were all part of the same criminal transaction. Some states have enacted statutes to prohibit such convictions. Other states apply the doctrine of merger or double jeopardy to eliminate the multiple convictions.

rules.rule_statement: At common law, the principal was called the principal in the first degree. A principal is the person whose act or omissions are the actus reus of the crime. An accessory who was actually or constructively present at the scene of the crime was called the principal in the second degree. rules.rule_statement: A principal is the person whose acts or omissions are the actus reus of the crime, in other words, the perpetrator of the crime. The principal must be actually or constructively present at the scene of the crime. A principal is constructively present when some instrumentality he left or controlled resulted in the commission of the crime. If two or more people are directly responsible for the actus reus, they are joint principals (i.e., coprincipals).

rules.rule_statement: An accomplice is a person who, with the requisite mens rea, aids or abets a principal prior to or during the commission of the crime. In general, the mental state needed for accomplice liability is the intent to assist the principal in committing the underlying crime. However, if the underlying crime requires recklessness or negligence as a mens rea, most jurisdictions require only this lesser type of mens rea for accomplice liability for the crime.

rules.rule_statement: Some states draw a distinction between an accessory before the fact and a principal in the second degree based upon presence at the scene of the crime. An accomplice who is physically or constructively present during the commission of the crime is a principal in the second degree. For example, a getaway driver some distance from the scene is deemed constructively present and will be considered a principal in the second degree. An accomplice who is neither physically nor constructively present during the commission of the crime, but who possesses the requisite intent, is an accessory before the fact. For example, someone who helped plan the crime or acquired tools or weapons necessary to commit the crime would still be considered an accomplice even if the person was not physically or constructively present during the commission of the crime.

rules.rule_statement: A majority of states follow the MPC rule for an accomplice’s mental state. Under the MPC, a person is an accomplice in the commission of an offense if he acts with the purpose of promoting or facilitating the commission of the offense. The accomplice must solicit, aid, agree, or attempt to aid in the planning or commission of the crime, with the intent that the crime actually be committed. MPC § 2.06(3). Mere knowledge that another person intends to commit a crime is not enough to make a person an accomplice. A minority of states hold a person liable as an accomplice when the person intentionally or knowingly aids, induces, or causes another person to commit an offense. Under the minority rule, any voluntary act that actually assists or encourages the principal in a known criminal aim is sufficient for accomplice liability even if the person does not act with the intent of aiding the commission of the crime. In jurisdictions that have adopted the majority rule, a person encouraging or assisting a criminal who is not guilty of the crime itself as an accomplice may be guilty of a lesser crime, such as criminal facilitation. When the crime committed by the principal only requires the principal to act recklessly or negligently (e.g., involuntary manslaughter), a person may be an accomplice to that crime under the majority rule when the person merely acts recklessly or negligently with regard to the principal’s commission of the crime, rather than purposefully or intentionally.

rules.rule_statement: An accomplice is responsible for the crime to the same extent as the principal. If the principal commits crimes other than the crimes for which the accomplice has provided encouragement or assistance, then the accomplice is liable for the other crimes when the crimes are the natural and probable consequences of the accomplice’s conduct. Example: Party D encouraged E to burn V’s house and E did so. The fire spread to W’s house, and it was foreseeable that it would do so. Party D is an accomplice to the burning of W’s house. An accomplice may be criminally liable even though she cannot be a principal. Example: A woman who could not commit rape at common law as a principal could be criminally liable for rape if she aided the male principal in the crime (e.g., restraining the victim for the principal).

Accomplice’S Criminal Liability
An accomplice is responsible for the crime to the same extent as the principal. If the principal commits crimes other than the crimes for which the accomplice has provided encouragement or assistance, then the accomplice is liable for the other crimes when the crimes are the natural and probable consequences of the accomplice’s conduct. Example: Party D encouraged E to burn V’s house and E did so. The fire spread to W’s house, and it was foreseeable that it would do so. Party D is an accomplice to the burning of W’s house. An accomplice may be criminally liable even though she cannot be a principal. Example: A woman who could not commit rape at common law as a principal could be criminally liable for rape if she aided the male principal in the crime (e.g., restraining the victim for the principal).

rules.rule_statement: Insanity encompasses mental abnormalities that may affect legal responsibility. It is a legal term rather than a psychiatric term. The four legal tests for insanity expressly exclude defendants diagnosed as sociopathic or psychopathic. Such individuals have a tendency to commit antisocial and sometimes violent acts and are incapable of experiencing guilt. The fact that a defendant has such tendencies does not necessarily mean that the defendant has the requisite mental state for an insanity defense. A defendant who puts sanity at issue can be compelled to submit to psychiatric testing. The defendant could be so compelled after being informed of his Fifth Amendment rights.

rules.canonical_name: The Nature And Quality Of The Act;
rules.rule_statement: ii) The wrongfulness of the act. Without knowing that an act is wrong, a defendant could not have formed the requisite criminal intent. Therefore, it is important to assess whether the defendant’s mental disease or condition prevented the defendant from understanding “right from wrong.” For example, if a defendant had delusional thoughts and committed an unlawful act, consider whether those thoughts prevented the defendant from understanding the wrongfulness of such an act. However, a defendant is not necessarily exculpated simply because he believes his acts to be morally right, although a few states do allow for such a defense. Notably, loss of control because of mental illness is not a defense under this test.

rules.rule_statement: In the majority of jurisdictions, the defendant has the burden of proving insanity. The level of proof required in these jurisdictions can be either (i) a preponderance of the evidence or (ii) clear and convincing evidence. Other jurisdictions require the defendant to overcome the presumption of sanity by introducing evidence of the defendant’s insanity. In these jurisdictions, there is then a shift in the burden of persuasion to the prosecution, which must prove beyond a reasonable doubt that the defendant is sane.

In many jurisdictions, the burden of proof about incompetence to stand trial is statutorily placed on the defendant.

Intoxication occurs when a person has diminished physical or mental control because of the effects of alcohol or drugs. Depending on the circumstances, a defendant may claim an intoxication defense to avoid criminal responsibility. There are two types of intoxication defenses: Voluntary ii. Involuntary

rules.rule_statement: Voluntary intoxication is the intentional taking of a substance known to be intoxicating; actual intoxication need not be intended. Voluntary intoxication is a defense to specific-intent crimes when the intoxication prevents the formation of the required intent. For example, intoxication may prevent the formation of the premeditation required for first-degree murder but not second-degree murder. Under the MPC, voluntary intoxication is a defense to crimes for which a material element requires a mental state that is purposely or knowingly. The intoxication must prevent the formation of that mental state. Voluntary intoxication is not a defense when the intent was formed before intoxication or when the defendant becomes intoxicated to establish the defense of voluntary intoxication. Voluntary intoxication is not a defense to crimes involving malice, recklessness, or negligence. Voluntary intoxication is also not a defense to strict-liability crimes.

Voluntary Intoxication
Voluntary intoxication is the intentional taking of a substance known to be intoxicating; actual intoxication need not be intended. Voluntary intoxication is a defense to specific-intent crimes when the intoxication prevents the formation of the required intent. For example, intoxication may prevent the formation of the premeditation required for first-degree murder but not second-degree murder. Under the MPC, voluntary intoxication is a defense to crimes for which a material element requires a mental state that is purposely or knowingly. The intoxication must prevent the formation of that mental state. Voluntary intoxication is not a defense when the intent was formed before intoxication or when the defendant becomes intoxicated to establish the defense of voluntary intoxication. Voluntary intoxication is not a defense to crimes involving malice, recklessness, or negligence. Voluntary intoxication is also not a defense to strict-liability crimes.

rules.rule_statement: Involuntary intoxication is a defense when the intoxication serves to negate an element of the crime. Involuntary intoxication applies to general- and specific-intent crimes as well as malice crimes. In addition, involuntary intoxication may be a defense to a strict-liability crime by negating the required actus reus (i.e., voluntary act). To be considered involuntary, the intoxicating substance must have been taken: i) Without knowledge of the intoxicating nature of the substance (including substances taken pursuant to medical advice); or ii) Under duress. Although intoxication and insanity are two separate defenses, excessive drinking and drug use may cause a person to experience insanity. Thus, intoxication can give rise to an insanity defense when the requirements for the defense are met.

rules.rule_statement: At common law, a child under the age of seven could not be convicted of a crime. A child at least seven years old but less than 14 years old was rebuttably presumed to be incapable of committing a crime. A child at least 14 years old could be charged with a crime as an adult. Modern statutes have modified this rule and provide that no child can be convicted of a crime until a certain age is reached, usually between the ages of 11 and 14.

Modern statutes have modified this rule and provide that no child can be convicted of a crime until a certain age is reached, usually between the ages of 11 and 14.

rules.rule_statement: To prove a homicide, the prosecution must show that the defendant caused the victim’s death. The prosecution must prove both actual and proximate causation.
rules.short_rule_statement: To prove a homicide, the prosecution must show that the defendant caused the victim’s death. The prosecution must prove both actual and proximate causation.
rules.source_text:
Causation
To prove a homicide, the prosecution must show that the defendant caused the victim’s death. The prosecution must prove both actual and proximate causation.

rules.rule_statement: If a victim would not have died but for the defendant’s act, then the defendant’s act is the actual cause (i.e., cause-in-fact) of the death. When the defendant sets in motion forces that lead to the death of the victim, the defendant is the actual cause of the victim’s death. Example: A mechanical device set up by the defendant killed an individual. The defendant is considered to have caused that individual’s death. Actual causation can be found when there are multiple causes, (i.e., other persons are also responsible for the victim’s death). In cases involving multiple causes, the defendant’s act must have been a substantial factor in causing the victim’s death. Simultaneous acts by different individuals who are acting independently may each be considered the actual cause of a victim’s death even though the victim would have died in the absence of one of the acts. Example: Two individuals simultaneously shot a third individual. Either of the shots would have killed the victim. Each shot is considered the actual cause of the victim’s death. A defendant’s act is not the cause of death when a victim was killed by an independent cause before the defendant’s act could kill the victim. Example: Party A planned to kill B by stabbing him. When A approached B in bed, B was in a nonresponsive state. Party A assumed that B was asleep and stabbed him multiple times. In actuality, however, B had died one hour previously due to a massive heart attack. Party A’s actions did not cause B’s death. Therefore, there is no homicide of B, but A may be charged with the attempted murder of B. A victim’s preexisting condition that contributes to the victim’s death does not supplant the defendant’s conduct as an actual cause of the victim’s death. Example: A victim had a heart condition. The defendant hit the victim with a club, intending to kill the victim. The blow would not have killed the victim but for the victim’s heart condition. Nevertheless, the defendant’s actions were the actual cause of the victim’s death. Providing a person with the means to commit suicide generally does not make the provider guilty of murder as an accomplice (because suicide is not homicide). Instead, the person who provided the means may be guilty of a lesser crime, such as assisting a suicide. Consent is not a defense to homicide. Therefore, a “mercy killing” (i.e., euthanasia) can be a criminal homicide even if the person was willing to die because of a painful terminal illness.

rules.rule_statement: Proximate cause (i.e., legal cause) exists only when a defendant is deemed legally responsible for a homicide. For the defendant to be legally responsible for a homicide, the death must be foreseeable. A death caused by the defendant’s conduct is deemed foreseeable if death is the natural and probable result of the conduct. An act that accelerates death is a legal cause of that death. Actions by a force of nature that are not within the defendant’s control are generally not foreseeable (e.g., a lightning strike that kills a victim the defendant tied to a tree). EXAM NOTE: Proximate cause is commonly tested in the context of the felony-murder rule.  A frequently applied standard is that the homicide must be a natural and probable consequence of the defendant’s actions. Even if there is some intervening act, the defendant will still be held responsible unless the intervening act was so out-of-the-ordinary that it would be unjust to hold the defendant criminally responsible for the outcome. As the MPC explains it, when the injury suffered is generally of the type intended by the defendant, proximate causation exists if the injury is “not too remote or accidental in its occurrence to have a [just] bearing on the actor’s liability or on the gravity of his offense.” Actions by a victim (e.g., suicide to escape the pain that resulted from the injuries inflicted by the defendant) are typically foreseeable. In addition, actions by a third party (e.g., negligence by the doctor treating the victim) are generally foreseeable. Proximate Cause
Proximate cause (i.e., legal cause) exists only when a defendant is deemed legally responsible for a homicide. For the defendant to be legally responsible for a homicide, the death must be foreseeable. A death caused by the defendant’s conduct is deemed foreseeable if death is the natural and probable result of the conduct. An act that accelerates death is a legal cause of that death. Actions by a force of nature that are not within the defendant’s control are generally not foreseeable (e.g., a lightning strike that kills a victim the defendant tied to a tree). EXAM NOTE: Proximate cause is commonly tested in the context of the felony-murder rule.  A frequently applied standard is that the homicide must be a natural and probable consequence of the defendant’s actions. rules.rule_statement: When accompanied by a defendant’s intent to kill, conduct that is the legal cause of the death of a living person constitutes intent-to-kill murder. An inference of intent to kill may be made if a deadly weapon was used intentionally in the commission of the crime. Example: Party A intended to kill Party B. By the act of shooting B, A killed B. Party A would be criminally liable for intent-to-kill murder of B.

rules.canonical_name: Intent To Inﬂict Serious Bodily Harm
rules.rule_statement: A person who intends to do serious bodily injury or grievous bodily harm but actually succeeds in killing is guilty of murder despite the lack of intent to kill. Intent to inflict serious bodily harm can be inferred from the use of a deadly weapon to inflict bodily injury. Example: Party A intentionally hit Party B over the head with a baseball bat, intending to hurt B but not kill him. Later, B died from a skull fracture. Even though A did not intend to kill B, A would be guilty of murder because A had the intent to inflict serious bodily harm.

rules.rule_statement: Depraved-heart murder is an unintentional killing that results in death. A killing that results from reckless indifference to an unjustifiably high risk to human life is a depraved-heart murder. Example: Party A stood on a highway overpass and, as a joke, dropped a bowling ball from the overpass into oncoming traffic. Party A’s conduct resulted in the death of Party B, a passing motorist. Party A acted with a depraved heart and is subject to criminal liability for killing even though A did not have the intent to kill B. There is a split among jurisdictions as to whether the requisite depravity exists when a defendant was actually unaware of the risk involved in the conduct. The majority of states and the MPC impose liability only when the defendant actually realizes the danger. The majority rule is a subjective standard. By contrast, a minority of states use an objective standard. Under this standard, liability is imposed if a reasonable person would have recognized the danger. Even in those states that ordinarily follow a subjective standard, a conviction for depraved-heart murder will stand if the reason the defendant failed to appreciate the risk was due to voluntary intoxication. Example: Generally, reckless driving alone will not lead to a charge of depraved-heart murder. Such a charge would be appropriate only if the reckless driving were extreme, such as if it were combined with intoxication or other aggravating factors.

rules.rule_statement: Felony murder is an unintended killing proximately caused by and during the commission or attempted commission of an inherently dangerous felony. To convict a defendant of felony murder, the prosecution must establish that the underlying felony was either attempted or completed. EXAM NOTE: The mnemonic BARRK can be used to remember the felonies traditionally considered inherently dangerous: Burglary Arson Rape Robbery Kidnapping Common-law felonies also include murder, manslaughter, mayhem, and sodomy, but the BARRK crimes are most commonly tested in the context of a felony murder.  Neither murder nor manslaughter can be the basis for a felony-murder charge. In most states, any aggravated felony committed with the use of a dangerous weapon is subject to the felony-murder rule. However, such an aggravated felony must be independent of the killing itself to qualify as an underlying felony for felony murder. For example, aggravated battery cannot be the basis for a felony-murder charge. Example: Party A accidentally shot a homeowner while committing a burglary. Party A can be charged with felony murder. Generally, cofelons (including accessories) are vicariously liable for the death if the death is a foreseeable consequence of the underlying inherently dangerous felony There is no charge of attempted felony murder if the unintended victim does not die. See also § 5). Killing by a Felony Victim or Police Officer, below. The underlying felony will generally “merge” into the crime of felony murder for the purposes of double jeopardy. That is, the predicate felony is generally deemed a lesser included offense of felony murder. For example, in the majority of jurisdictions, a defendant who kills the proprietor of a store while committing a robbery can be punished only for felony murder; the robbery conviction would “merge” into the felony-murder conviction. A minority of jurisdictions have enacted statutes explicitly allowing cumulative punishment for both the felony murder and the underlying felony. While these statutes have been held to pass constitutional muster, for purposes of the bar exam, you should assume that cumulative punishment violates the Double Jeopardy Clause. Any of the following circumstances can constitute a defense to a felony-murder charge:

rules.rule_statement: When someone other than a cofelon is killed by a police officer or dies as a result of resistance by the victim of the felony, the felon’s liability for that death will depend on whether an agency theory or proximate-cause theory is applied. Under an agency theory, the felon will not be liable for the death of a bystander caused by a felony victim or police officer because neither person is the felon’s agent. Under the proximate-cause theory, liability for the bystander’s death may attach to the felon because the death is a direct consequence of the felony. The same analysis is applicable when a victim of the underlying felony or a police officer is killed by someone who is not one of the felons. EXAM NOTE: Agency theory is the majority position and should be applied on the MBE unless the question speciﬁcally indicates that the jurisdiction is a proximate-cause theory jurisdiction.  Proximate-cause theory is the minority position and subjects a defendant to liability for felony murder when the death is a direct consequence of the felony.

Under the Redline doctrine, a defendant is generally not guilty of felony murder when a victim or a police officer, acting in self-defense or trying to prevent the escape of the defendant or his cofelon, kills the cofelon. Instead, the killing by the victim or the police officer is considered justifiable homicide.

rules.rule_statement: First-degree murder is generally defined as a deliberate and premeditated murder. Defined in this manner, first-degree murder is a specific intent crime, which means that specific intent defenses are available for a defendant (see § I.B.1. Specific-Intent Crimes, supra). In addition, felony murder is frequently classified as first-degree murder. First-degree murder generally consists of two types of murder: · Deliberate and premediated murder · Felony murder Because the specific criteria for first-degree murder are established only by statute, a homicide cannot be first-degree murder without a corresponding statute. The distinguishing element of first-degree murder is premeditation, meaning the defendant reflected on the idea of killing or planned the killing. The amount of time needed for premeditation may be brief. After forming the intent to kill, the defendant must have had sufficient time to become fully conscious of the intent and to consider the killing (i.e., had time for reflection). This requirement does not apply to felony murder. If a murder is committed during the perpetration of an enumerated felony, then it may be first-degree murder. The most commonly enumerated felonies are Burglary, Arson, Rape, Robbery, and Kidnapping. [Mnemonic: BARRK]. A homicide committed during the commission of an inherently dangerous felony may be treated as first- or second-degree murder depending upon the jurisdiction. A murder resulting from an egregious act may be classified as first-degree murder. Examples of an egregious act include ambush (i.e., lying in wait), torture, bombing, terrorism, and poisoning.

The defendant cannot have been set off by something that would not bother most people. rules.canonical_name: Subjective Test: The Defendant Acted In The Heat Of Passion;
rules.rule_statement: ii) Objective test: An ordinary person would have acted in the heat of passion. A serious battery, a threat of deadly force, or discovery of adultery by a spouse constitutes adequate provocation. In most jurisdictions, simply hearing about a spouse’s affair is not sufficient provocation, but other facts and circumstances may support a finding of adequate provocation. Usually mere words, such as taunts, are not adequate provocation. However, words that convey inflammatory or offensive information may cause an immediate and intense emotional response that is sufficient. While an intentional killing committed when resisting arrest is generally murder, the intentional killing can be manslaughter if the arrest was unlawful and the defendant acted in the “heat of passion.” If there was sufficient time between the provocation and the killing for a reasonable person to cool off, then murder is not mitigated to manslaughter. Even if the defendant has cooled off, a second encounter with the victim may give rise to another situation in which the defendant acts in the “heat of passion.” There must be a causal connection between the provocation, the heat of passion, and the fatal act. There will be no mitigation when the intent to kill was formed prior to the provocation. When, because of a reasonable mistake of fact, the defendant is in error in identifying her provoker, or accidentally kills the wrong person, she will be guilty of voluntary manslaughter if that would have been her crime had she killed the provoker. However, if the defendant, in her passion, intentionally kills another person known to her to be an innocent bystander, then there will be no mitigation. Instead, the defendant will be criminally liable for murder, not voluntary manslaughter.

rules.rule_statement: Criminal negligence is grossly negligent conduct that puts another person at a significant risk of serious bodily injury or death. It requires more than the type of ordinary negligence required for tort liability but consists of something less than the extremely reckless conduct required for depraved-heart murder. For example, the failure of a parent, under a duty of care, to provide medical care to a sick minor child constitutes criminal negligence. Under the MPC, the defendant must have acted recklessly, which is a “gross deviation from the standard of conduct that a law-abiding person would observe in the actor’s situation.” The defendant must have been actually aware of the risk his conduct posed.

Criminal Negligence
Criminal negligence is grossly negligent conduct that puts another person at a significant risk of serious bodily injury or death. It requires more than the type of ordinary negligence required for tort liability but consists of something less than the extremely reckless conduct required for depraved-heart murder. For example, the failure of a parent, under a duty of care, to provide medical care to a sick minor child constitutes criminal negligence. Under the MPC, the defendant must have acted recklessly, which is a “gross deviation from the standard of conduct that a law-abiding person would observe in the actor’s situation.” The defendant must have been actually aware of the risk his conduct posed.

rules.rule_statement: The unlawful act may occur in one of two ways: i) Under the misdemeanor-manslaughter rule, a killing committed in the commission of a malum in se (wrong in itself) misdemeanor; or ii) A killing committed in the commission of a felony that is not statutorily treated as first-degree felony murder or second-degree murder. Malum in se means “wrong in itself” or “inherently evil” and includes crimes such as assault and battery. Malum prohibitum refers to wrongs that are merely prohibited (i.e., not inherently immoral or hurtful, but wrong because of a statute), such as a parking violation, smuggling, or failure to obtain a license. A homicide resulting from a malum prohibitum act generally does not qualify as an “unlawful act” under the misdemeanor-manslaughter rule in most jurisdictions. However, a malum prohibitum act may constitute involuntary manslaughter if the unlawful act was willful or constituted criminal negligence.

rules.rule_statement: Crimes against property consist of the following types of offenses: Larceny and larceny by trick Forgery Embezzlement False pretenses Robbery Extortion Burglary Arson Possession offenses Receipt of stolen property Make sure to understand the main difference between embezzlement and larceny. For embezzlement, a defendant must have been legally entrusted with the property by the owner and then fraudulently convert the property to the defendant’s own use. With larceny, the initial taking must have been trespassory. There cannot have been lawful entrustment by the owner in a larceny case.

rules.rule_statement: The taking requirement is satisfied by any trespassory removal of the property from the owner’s possession into another’s control. The taking requirement is also known as “caption.” The destruction of property while it was in the owner’s possession (e.g., breaking an object held by the owner) is not a taking. A taking occurs if the defendant used an agent to remove the property from the owner’s possession. The agent does not need to be aware of the defendant’s criminal intent.

Taking—Caption
The taking requirement is satisfied by any trespassory removal of the property from the owner’s possession into another’s control. The taking requirement is also known as “caption.” The destruction of property while it was in the owner’s possession (e.g., breaking an object held by the owner) is not a taking. A taking occurs if the defendant used an agent to remove the property from the owner’s possession. The agent does not need to be aware of the defendant’s criminal intent.

rules.rule_statement: The requirement of a carrying away is satisfied by even a slight movement of the property (e.g., inches). This requirement is also known as “asportation.” Most courts will impute another person’s asportation to the defendant if the person acted at the defendant’s direction. Example: The defendant purported to sell a vehicle that he did not own to an innocent buyer. After the purchase, the buyer drove the vehicle away. The buyer’s asportation of the vehicle is imputed to the defendant in most jurisdictions. The MPC has eliminated the asportation requirement and instead focuses on the defendant’s exercise of unlawful control over the property.

rules.rule_statement: The property taken must be personal property, not real property. Electricity or gas supplied by a utility constitutes personal property. Intangible property will not suffice. However, documents that represent the rights to intangible property (e.g., stocks, bonds) are treated as personal property. Modern theft statutes usually criminalize obtaining services without paying for them (i.e., the theft of services). EXAM NOTE: Services taken without paying for the services (e.g., a massage) do not constitute personal property for the purposes of the MBE. The taking of fixtures (i.e., items affixed to real property) or real property items (e.g., trees, unharvested crops) is not larceny when the defendant’s act of severance occurs immediately before the carrying away of the fixture or other real property items. However, when the real property items (e.g., picked apples) have previously been severed from the land by the owner, they become personal property and the carrying away of such items can be larceny.

rules.rule_statement: The property must be in the possession of someone other than the defendant. The owner of property (i.e., a person who has title to it) can commit larceny when someone other than the owner (e.g., a lessee) is entitled to current possession of the property. To rightfully have “current possession,” the possessor must have the legal right to possess the property (e.g., a lease); it is not enough for the possessor to merely borrow the property from the owner. Larceny may even be committed against a thief. The taking of stolen property from a thief can constitute larceny unless the taker has a superior possessory interest in the property (e.g., an owner or a lessee of the property). A joint owner of property who takes possession of the property from a co?owner is not guilty of larceny because the taker has an equal right to possess the property. Constructive possession means legal possession when factual possession does not exist. An owner has constructive possession of property when actual possession, but not title, is taken from her by fraud. The crime is called larceny by trick. See § III.A.2. Larceny by Trick, infra.

rules.rule_statement: Low-level employees can only be guilty of larceny, whereas high-level employees are typically guilty of embezzlement. An employer generally has constructive possession of property in the hands of a lower-level employee. Such an employee has custody, not possession, of the employer’s property. A higher-level employee (e.g., company president) who has greater authority with respect to the employer’s property may have possession of, rather than custody of, such property and may be guilty of embezzlement (not larceny) for taking the property.

rules.rule_statement: ii) The finder possesses the necessary intent to permanently deprive the owner of the property. Property that has been mistakenly delivered may be the subject of larceny when: ii) The recipient possesses the necessary intent to permanently deprive the owner of the property. When a defendant takes legitimate possession of an item, but he discovers another item enclosed in the larger item (the container), there is an issue of whether the defendant had possession of the enclosed item at the time the defendant legitimately possessed the larger item. If so, there is no larceny because the defendant has not taken the property from another’s possession. However, determining possession is a difficult task. Larceny may depend on whether the parties intended to transfer the container. If the intent is to transfer, then no larceny is committed because the defendant effectively takes immediate possession of both items. A few states hold that the defendant does not take possession of the enclosed property until he discovers it, at which point, if he forms the intent to keep the property, he is guilty of larceny.

rules.rule_statement: If the defendant intends only to borrow the property with the ability to do so, then larceny does not occur because there is no intent to permanently deprive the owner of the property (e.g., borrowing a car to run an errand). If property is taken with the intent to return the property and is accidentally damaged or destroyed, larceny has not occurred. Example: Party D took Party A’s car to run an errand. On the way back to A’s house, D was in a car accident and A’s car was totaled. Party D is not guilty of larceny because D did not intend to permanently deprive A of her car.

rules.rule_statement: Larceny does not occur when a defendant takes property under a mistaken but good faith belief that the specific property belongs to the defendant under a claim of right. This is because the requisite intent to deprive the victim of ownership is absent. (Note that a claim of right cannot serve as a justification for robbery in most states. See § III.A.6.e.) Larceny also does not occur if a defendant takes money with the honest belief of being entitled to the money as repayment of a debt. However, a claim-of-right defense to larceny is precluded when a defendant takes property owned by the debtor with the intent to hold it until the debt is repaid, because the defendant is not acting pursuant to a perceived ownership right in that property.

However, a claim-of-right defense to larceny is precluded when a defendant takes property owned by the debtor with the intent to hold it until the debt is repaid, because the defendant is not acting pursuant to a perceived ownership right in that property.

rules.rule_statement: A defendant intending to return property in expectation of claiming a reward has not committed larceny unless his intent is to return the property only upon receiving the reward. There is sufficient intent if the defendant intends to create a substantial risk of loss of the property or if the defendant intends to sell the goods back to the owner. The intent to permanently deprive is generally measured at the time of the taking. The continuing-trespass rule may apply to stretch the time at which intent is measured. See § III.A.1.a. Trespass, supra.

rules.rule_statement: Unlike larceny, larceny by trick requires that the property be converted. Property is converted when the defendant, in a manner so serious as to deprive the victim of the use of the property, deprives the victim of possession of the property or interferes with the property. The deprivation must be substantial enough to justify a court to order the defendant to pay the full fair market value of the property.

A conversion pursuant to a claim of right also is not embezzlement.

If the defendant subjectively believes that he owns the property in question, he will not be guilty of false pretenses. rules.rule_statement: ii) Simultaneously with the taking; or iii) Immediately following the taking. The force used by the defendant must be more than the amount necessary to cause a taking and carrying away of the property. When a pickpocket takes the victim’s property without the victim’s knowledge, the taking does not constitute robbery unless the victim notices the taking and resists. Similarly, most state courts that have considered the issue have said that purse-snatching is not robbery unless additional circumstances transform the larceny into a robbery. Such circumstances are present when the victim notices the taking and resists, or when the victim is intimidated, knocked down, struck, or injured by greater force than is required to carry away the property. EXAM NOTE: The MBE often tests the degree of force necessary for robbery.  Remember that slight force is sufﬁcient.  The common law requires that the force must be manifested immediately before or at the same time as the taking.   The modern trend in many jurisdictions is that the force may be immediately following the taking (e.g., during escape). There must be a threat of immediate serious physical injury to the victim, a close family member, or other person present. A threat to damage or destroy property, other than the victim’s home, is generally not sufficient.

rules.rule_statement: At common law, a defendant was not guilty of robbery if he used force to take his own property from another (as the requisite intent to deprive the victim of ownership was absent). This defense was available as long as the defendant had a good faith belief that the property was his own, even if the belief was mistaken. Some states still recognize this defense, though most of those limit its applicability to a defendant’s taking of specific property to which the defendant has a legally recognizable title. The modern trend, however, is to reject the claim of right defense in the case of robbery, as public policy discourages the use of forcible self-help.

rules.rule_statement: A dwelling is a structure regularly occupied for habitation. It need not be occupied at the time of the breaking, but the structure must not be abandoned. All states have statutes that expand the type of structure to include nondwellings, such as businesses, buildings, or cars, and surrounding areas, such as yards. The dwelling must be that of another person. A person cannot burglarize his own dwelling, but the owner of a dwelling who has transferred the possessory interest to another (e.g., a tenant) can be guilty of burglary.

rules.rule_statement: Nighttime occurs during the period of darkness between sunset and sunrise. It is not considered nighttime if there is sufficient natural daylight to see the burglar’s face. Many states impose more severe penalties on nighttime burglaries.

rules.rule_statement: At the time of the breaking and entering, the defendant must have the intent to commit a felony (e.g., larceny, robbery, rape, murder) inside the dwelling. Many states have broadened the scope of the crimes intended to be committed to include misdemeanor thefts. A defendant who fails to commit the underlying felony may nevertheless be guilty of burglary as well as attempt to commit the underlying felony. If the underlying felony is completed, it does not merge with the burglary.

rules.rule_statement: Ownership is not required. The test is whether a person has the right to possession or occupancy of the dwelling. Many states have expanded arson to include the burning of one’s own dwelling. At common law, the burning of one’s own dwelling that was located near other houses or in a city was only a misdemeanor, and burning one’s own building for insurance fraud was not considered arson. Most states have expanded arson to include the burning of buildings other than dwellings, but burning contents in a building alone does not constitute arson.

rules.rule_statement: The MPC expands the crime of arson to include any intentional fire or explosion caused to destroy a building or occupied structure (including vehicles and businesses) or destroying or damaging any property to collect insurance on the loss. rules.rule_statement: When there is consent to sexual intercourse, rape has not occurred. Consent does not exist if intercourse is procured by force or threat of harm, or when the victim is unable to consent due to a drug-induced stupor or unconsciousness. Consent is ineffective if a victim consents to sexual intercourse because of a threat of harm, although the harm threatened must be imminent and must involve bodily harm. Economic duress is not sufficient. Fraud rarely negates consent. Consent obtained by fraud regarding the nature of the act itself—fraud in factum (e.g., the defendant convinces the victim that the act is not intercourse but part of a medical exam)—is not a valid defense. Consent obtained by fraud in the inducement (e.g., a promise of marriage in exchange for sex) is a valid defense. Resistance of the victim is not required but can be evidence of the victim’s lack of consent.

rules.rule_statement: Traditionally, under the doctrine of merger, if a person’s conduct constitutes both a felony and a misdemeanor, then the misdemeanor merges into the felony. In such a case, the person could be convicted of the felony but not the misdemeanor. However, if the crimes are of the same degree (i.e., all felonies or all misdemeanors), then there is no merger of the crimes. Modern law does not subscribe to the doctrine of merger based on the felony-misdemeanor distinction. However, modern law does apply the merger doctrine to solicitation and attempt and the solicited or completed crime. A defendant may be tried, but not punished, for (i) solicitation and the completed crime or (ii) attempt and the completed crime. Solicitation and attempt are said to “merge” into the completed crime. Under the MPC, a defendant may be concurrently prosecuted, but not punished, for more than one inchoate offense (i.e., solicitation, conspiracy, and attempt) based on conduct designed to culminate in the commission of the same crime. At common law, while solicitation merges into conspiracy if the person solicited agrees to commit the crime, conspiracy and attempt do not merge. Be sure to understand how the merger doctrine applies to the three inchoate offenses. Unlike a conviction for either solicitation or attempt, a conviction for conspiracy does not merge into a conviction for the completed crime.

rules.rule_statement: Factual impossibility is not a defense because the solicitation is completed at the moment of enticement, even if the crime is not capable of being completed. However, if the solicitor is part of a group that is exempted from criminal culpability by a statute, then the solicitor cannot be guilty of solicitation for the statutory crime. For example, a minor soliciting sex cannot be guilty of solicitation for statutory rape. Example: Party A goes into a bar and asks B to kill A’s husband. If B says no, A can be charged with the crime of solicitation. If B says yes, A can be charged with conspiracy to commit murder. If B actually kills A’s husband, the solicitation charge would merge into the underlying murder charge. Party A could then be charged with both conspiracy to commit murder (which does not merge) and murder, but not solicitation (which does merge).

rules.rule_statement: Conspiracy is a specific-intent crime. A conspirator must have the intent to agree and the intent to commit the criminal objective. The intent to agree may be inferred from the conduct of the parties. Because agreement and intent to commit the crime are elements of conspiracy, criminal liability for a conspiracy cannot be based solely on knowledge of the existence of the conspiracy. Example: A merchant who supplies goods to a conspirator knowing that the conspirator intends to use the goods in furtherance of the objective of the conspiracy is not a member of the conspiracy simply because the merchant possessed such knowledge. Instead, the merchant must take an additional step to show criminal intent, such as selling the goods at an exorbitant price, basing the price of the goods on a percentage of the conspiracy’s “take,” or ordering specially manufactured goods that the merchant does not normally sell. Conspiracy to commit arson requires specific intent even though the substantive offense of arson only requires malice. Similarly, a conspiracy to commit a strict- liability crime requires intent.

rules.rule_statement: Persons who do not know each other can be members of the same conspiracy if there is a community of interest in the achievement of the objective of the conspiracy. A community of interest is usually found when the activities of each person resemble links of a chain, such as a scheme to acquire and distribute drugs. In such a conspiracy, all of the members of the community of interest are liable for the acts of the others in furtherance of the conspiracy. A scheme that resembles a hub with spokes is less likely to have a community of interest. An example of such a scheme is the processing of fraudulent loans by one person that were submitted by numerous other individuals. In such a case, the “hub” and each “spoke” are usually treated as having formed a separate conspiracy from all of the other hub-spoke combinations. Thus, the common hub will be liable for all of the conspiracies, but the spoke members are not liable for the acts of the other conspirators.

rules.rule_statement: When it is factually impossible to complete the intended crime, a defendant cannot claim factual impossibility as a defense to conspiracy. By contrast, if the intended act is not criminal in nature, then legal impossibility may be a defense to conspiracy when the objective of the conspiracy is not a crime. Example: Party A and Party B agree to steal a painting from a house at night when the owners are out of town. The painting turns out to be missing from the house on the night that A and B break and enter to steal it. Both parties will still be liable for conspiracy to commit burglary, despite the factual impossibility.

rules.rule_statement: At common law, withdrawal was not a defense to conspiracy because a conspiracy is complete as soon as the parties enter into the agreement. The majority rule, which is also the federal rule, provides that a conspiracy does not come into existence until an overt act has been committed. Consequently, after there has been an agreement but before an overt act has been committed, a person may avoid criminal liability for conspiracy by communicating notice of his intent not to participate to the other potential co-conspirators or by informing the police about the agreement. However, upon completion of the overt act, the conspiracy is formed, and withdrawal is no longer possible. Under the MPC and the minority view, subsequent withdrawal is possible only if the defendant acts voluntarily to “thwart the success” of the conspiracy.

rules.rule_statement: The “substantial-step” test is a subjective test. The test is applied to determine whether an attempt has occurred. Under this test, a person is guilty of an attempted crime if the person took a substantial step toward completing a crime. Conduct does not constitute a substantial step if it is done in mere preparation; the act must be conduct in furtherance of the crime.

rules.rule_statement: The defendant must possess the specific intent to perform an act or attain a result, which, if completed, would constitute the target crime, even if the target crime is not a specific-intent crime. Arson is not a specific-intent crime, but attempted arson is. An attempt to commit a strict-liability crime is also a specific-intent crime. There is no attempt to commit negligent crimes like involuntary manslaughter because a defendant’s act cannot be both intentional and negligent.

rules.rule_statement: Impossibility is not a defense to attempt when the crime attempted is factually impossible to commit due to circumstances unknown to the defendant. However, if the act intended is not a crime (i.e., a legal impossibility), then the defendant is not guilty of attempt. Even when statutes purport to have done away with impossibility as a defense in general, there is typically a provision that allows for legal impossibility as a defense. Example: Party A shot Party B, believing that B was sleeping. In actuality, B was already dead. Party A is guilty of attempted murder, but not murder.

rules.rule_statement: A defendant who does not complete a crime may be charged only with attempt of the completed crime. Punishment for attempt is usually less severe than the sentence for the completed offense. If a defendant completes a crime, the defendant may be charged with (i) attempt of the completed crime and (ii) the completed crime. However, in such cases, the defendant may be convicted of only one crime (either attempt or the completed crime).

rules.rule_statement: When a defendant is factually mistaken, the defendant may generally rely on a mistake-of-fact defense so long as the mistake is a reasonable one. For example, mistake of fact may be a defense for a defendant who injures a victim, thinking that the victim was holding a real pistol instead of a toy gun. A defendant’s physical characteristics, experiences, and knowledge are taken into account to determine the reasonableness of the defendant’s mistake.

rules.rule_statement: A person does not have an obligation to retreat before using nondeadly force. Under the majority view, retreat is not required even when deadly force is used in self-defense. A minority of states follow the retreat doctrine. Under the retreat doctrine, retreat is required if it can be safely accomplished. Even under the minority view, however, retreat is never required when the person employing deadly force is in his own home. The doctrine authorizing the use of deadly force in one’s home without the need for retreat is known as the castle doctrine.
