rules.rule_statement: Imperfect self-defense occurs when the person claiming self-defense unjustifiably kills the attacker. The purpose of the imperfect self-defense rule is to reduce a murder charge to voluntary manslaughter. Adopted in most states, the rule is applied when the defendant cannot claim perfect self-defense for some reason. For example, the defense may be used when a defendant honestly but unreasonably believed that deadly force was required to prevent death or serious bodily injury.

For example, the defense may be used when a defendant honestly but unreasonably believed that deadly force was required to prevent death or serious bodily injury.

rules.rule_statement: A person in lawful possession of property may take reasonable steps, including the use of nondeadly force, to protect the property when the person has no time to seek assistance from law enforcement. To use force, the defender must reasonably believe that the real property is in immediate danger of unlawful trespass or that personal property is in immediate danger of being carried away, and that the use of force is necessary to prevent either. The force cannot be unreasonably disproportionate to the perceived harm.

The force cannot be unreasonably disproportionate to the perceived harm.

rules.rule_statement: Deadly force cannot be used in defending property. There is one exception to this rule. A person may use deadly force to prevent or terminate forcible entry into a dwelling when the occupant reasonably believes that the intruder intends to commit a felony inside. The use of deadly force against an intruder exiting the dwelling is generally not permissible. A deadly mechanical device cannot be used to protect property, although nondeadly mechanical devices or objects (such as barbed wire) may be used. Any right to use deadly force in the protection of a dwelling is limited to the dwelling (i.e., one’s occupied residence). Thus, a gardening shed on the edge of the property would likely not be covered by this right. However, any room in the residence would be covered by the right.

rules.rule_statement: A citizen’s arrest occurs when a civilian makes an arrest but acts without police direction in making that arrest. A civilian cannot rely on this defense when he made a mistake, even a reasonable mistake, about the commission of the crime. However, a civilian may rely on the defense if he made a reasonable mistake about the identity of the perpetrator of the crime.

A private citizen who makes a mistake, even a reasonable mistake, about the commission of a serious felony by the victim is not entitled to rely on this defense.

rules.rule_statement: A defendant may claim a duress defense when a third party made an unlawful threat that caused the defendant to violate the law. The defendant’s violation of the law must have been based on a reasonable belief the only way to avoid death or serious bodily injury was to violate the law. The defendant’s fear of harm must have been both an actual fear and objectively reasonable. Duress is not a defense to intentional murder. A defendant charged with felony murder may claim duress as a defense to the underlying felony and avoid a conviction for felony murder.
rules.short_rule_statement: A defendant may claim a duress defense when a third party made an unlawful threat that caused the defendant to violate the law. The defendant’s violation of the law must have...
rules.source_text:
Duress
A defendant may claim a duress defense when a third party made an unlawful threat that caused the defendant to violate the law. The defendant’s violation of the law must have been based on a reasonable belief the only way to avoid death or serious bodily injury was to violate the law. The defendant’s fear of harm must have been both an actual fear and objectively reasonable. Duress is not a defense to intentional murder. A defendant charged with felony murder may claim duress as a defense to the underlying felony and avoid a conviction for felony murder.

rules.rule_statement: If forces of nature (e.g., storm, fire) caused a defendant to commit an act that would otherwise be a crime, the defendant’s act may be justified based upon necessity. The law prefers that, when facing two evils, a person will act to avoid the greater evil by choosing the lesser evil. For example, necessity may be used to justify a defendant’s destruction of another person’s property to prevent the spread of a fire. The MPC does not explicitly limit the defense of necessity to natural forces. While the defendant may escape criminal liability based on necessity, the defendant may be compelled by tort law to reimburse the victim for any losses. Keep in mind the difference between the defenses of necessity and duress. Necessity is a result of natural forces (i.e., not human actions). Duress results from human actions.

rules.source_text:
Necessity
If forces of nature (e.g., storm, fire) caused a defendant to commit an act that would otherwise be a crime, the defendant’s act may be justified based upon necessity. The law prefers that, when facing two evils, a person will act to avoid the greater evil by choosing the lesser evil. For example, necessity may be used to justify a defendant’s destruction of another person’s property to prevent the spread of a fire. The MPC does not explicitly limit the defense of necessity to natural forces. While the defendant may escape criminal liability based on necessity, the defendant may be compelled by tort law to reimburse the victim for any losses. Keep in mind the difference between the defenses of necessity and duress. Necessity is a result of natural forces (i.e., not human actions). Duress results from human actions.

rules.rule_statement: A defendant is not entitled to assert necessity when he set the natural forces in motion (e.g., set the fire). A defendant cannot assert necessity as a justification if there was a reasonably apparent noncriminal alternative to the defendant’s act. In addition, economic necessity does not justify theft. For example, an unemployed worker may not steal food from the grocery store.

rules.rule_statement: Entrapment occurs when a government official or agent (e.g., law enforcement officer) conceives and plans a criminal offense and then uses trickery, persuasion, or fraud to get a defendant to commit the offense. The entrapment defense requires a showing that the defendant would not have committed the offense except for the official’s trickery, persuasion, or fraud. If an officer merely offers an already predisposed person the opportunity to commit a crime, it is not entrapment. In other words, the defendant must lack any predisposition to commit the crime. Entrapment can occur through the use of an undercover agent but not by a private citizen. Traditionally, a defendant was precluded from using the entrapment defense if he denied his participation in the event. However, the modern trend allows a defendant to raise the defense of entrapment even while denying participation in an event.

rules.rule_statement: The MPC and a few states have adopted an objective approach to defining entrapment. Under the objective approach, the focus is on the government’s action and the effect those actions would have on a hypothetical innocent person. This approach requires the government official or agent to have induced or encouraged the defendant to commit a crime. The government inducement or encouragement must be by using methods of persuasion that create a substantial risk the crime would be committed by an otherwise law-abiding citizen.

The government inducement or encouragement must be by using methods of persuasion that create a substantial risk the crime would be committed by an otherwise law-abiding citizen.

rules.rule_statement: An alibi is a defense whereby a defendant denies his participation in a crime because he asserts that he was elsewhere when the alleged crime was committed. An alibi is not an affirmative defense; the defendant is not required to prove that he was elsewhere when the crime was committed. Instead, the burden remains on the prosecution to prove that the defendant was the person who committed the crime.

rules.source_text:
Alibi
An alibi is a defense whereby a defendant denies his participation in a crime because he asserts that he was elsewhere when the alleged crime was committed. An alibi is not an affirmative defense; the defendant is not required to prove that he was elsewhere when the crime was committed. Instead, the burden remains on the prosecution to prove that the defendant was the person who committed the crime.
